Laplace-type integrals of the form I(λ) :=
Z

Rd g(x)e−λf(x)dx
(1.1) are a fundamental object in asymptotic analysis and a ubiquitous tool for deriving tractable ap-
proximations to otherwise intractable quantities, such as normalizing constants and expectations.

When λ is large, these integrals are dominated by neighborhoods of minimizers of f, and Laplace
asymptotics yields explicit leading-order formulas and systematic higher-order corrections. Such
approximations underpin both rigorous analysis (e.g. sharp tail probabilities and free-energy ex-
pansions) and practical computation in settings where direct numerical integration is infeasible.
In the classical regime where the dimension d is fixed and λ →∞, Laplace’s method and its
higher-order refinements are well developed [4, Section 8.3], [44, Section 5, Chapter IX]. At the
opposite extreme, there is also an infinite-dimensional theory (e.g. on Wiener space) in which λ−1 plays the role of a small-noise parameter [2]. What remains comparatively less understood is the
intermediate regime in which the dimension grows with the large parameter. This “growing-d”
regime is now very common in modern statistics, where Laplace-type integrals (or ratios thereof)
appear as marginal likelihoods and posterior expectations in models whose parameter dimension
increases with sample size. See Section 1.2 for more details. Likewise, the absence of rigorously
justified “growing-d” expansions created a significant void in areas such as statistical physics,
Euclidean quantum field theories (QFT), and chemistry, where formal expansions have been in
use for a long time without adequate justification, see Section 1.1.
Recent work by the second author establishes a high-dimensional Laplace expansion of I(λ),
with explicit remainder control, under the scaling d2/λ →0 [22]. The proof centers around an
approximation of f in the exponent by its second-order Taylor expansion. Thus the expansion of
I(λ) is closely tied to the problem of approximating concentrating densities, of the form π ∝e−λf,
by Gaussian distributions. In fact, the condition d2 ≪λ arises in numerous contexts as a threshold
for Gaussian approximation accuracy. In the context of Gaussian approximation to posteriors in
Bayesian inference, d2 ≪λ appears in the works [21, 20, 17, 23]. The condition d2 ≪λ also arises in
proofs of high-dimensional Central Limit Theorems (CLTs); see [32, 46, 19]. In the CLT, λ (usually
denoted n) is the given number of i.i.d. d-dimensional random vectors, whose average is of interest.
In fact, the aforementioned works [22, 21, 32] establish lower bounds as well, proving d2/λ ≪1
is necessary for the Gaussian approximations to be accurate. Although some works have in fact
proved the CLT under much weaker conditions on d relative to λ, the Gaussian approximation
holds in a much weaker sense. See Section 3.1 and Remark 2 of [8] for an overview of this line of
work.
Thus to summarize, the regime d2/λ →0 arises as a critical threshold in numerous results on
approximating d-dimensional densities or integrals involving e−λf. Another critical threshold is
the regime d/λ →0, which is generically required for distributions ∝e−λf to concentrate near the
minimizer of f; see e.g. [12, Corollary 2.1] and [38, Section 4.1]. Clearly, if there is no concentration
around the minimizer of f, one should not expect an expansion to hold whose terms are determined
solely by the derivatives of g and f at this single point.
This leaves an intriguing intermediate region where d2/λ does not vanish or even diverges to
infinity, while d/λ still converges to zero. Due to concentration about the minimizer of f, one
expects that I(λ) in (1.1) can still be characterized by derivatives of f and g at the minimizer.
But it is unclear how to obtain a closed form approximation to the integral without leaning on the
tractable Gaussian integral obtained by replacing f with its second-order Taylor expansion. To
our knowledge, there has been no rigorously justified explicit Laplace-type approximation of I(λ)
in this intermediate regime.
In this paper we have achieved an important milestone by completely characterizing this largely
unexplored intermediate regime under natural local regularity and global growth conditions. We
derive an explicit asymptotic series approximating (1.1) such that for each expansion order L ≥1,
the remainder is negligible as long as dL+1/λL →0.
The series approximates the logarithm of the integral I(λ), and as we explain below, this is
precisely what allows us to push d above the
√

and mild global growth of f away from x⋆, we show that

(1.2) for some coefficients bk(f, g) that satisfy bk(f, g) = O(dk+1). If d ≲log λ, then additional factors of
log λ are present in the remainder. The sum with respect to k is omitted if L = 1, and we assumed
without loss of generality that g = 1 at x⋆. We prove that the coefficients bk can be derived from
formal (and well-known) cumulant expansions.

Significance.
A central significance of our result is that it advances the modern asymptotic anal-
ysis program of developing expansions of ubiquitous Laplace type integrals that remain accurate in
increasingly high-dimensional regimes. The best known results to date work under the assumption
d2/λ →0. In contrast, we extend the range of dimensions for which one can make precise asymp-
totic statements with explicit formulas until the very limit, because beyond d/λ →0 there is no
concentration any longer. In this sense, our work brings the program initiated by Laplace in the
1700s to its logical conclusion, by providing explicit asymptotics (with remainder control) up to the
threshold where Laplace-type concentration breaks down. We show that a simple analytic approx-
imation remains valid whenever dL+1/λL →0 for any L ≥1, even in the genuinely intermediate
region where d2/λ ̸→0 (indeed, where d2/λ may diverge), by identifying an explicit exponential
correction at the level of the log-integral.
The implications of our results across many areas of science and engineering are numerous. Two
particularly noteworthy applications deserve special mention.
The first application is in physics, including statistical physics and QFT, where Laplace-type in-
tegrals encode quantities of central importance. In the high-dimensional, many-degrees-of-freedom
regimes relevant to these fields, such quantities are often evaluated via formal Laplace expansions,
typically without remainder bounds that remain valid in the joint limit of a large parameter and
growing dimension; see Section 1.1 for details. This theoretical gap, which dates back at least
to the Darwin–Fowler steepest-descent approach in 1922 [9], has long limited the rigor of many
computations. Our results fill the century-old gap by placing these Laplace calculations on
firm mathematical footing for a broad class of finite-dimensional large-system models.
Another area where our results are of significant importance is statistics. Here, λ plays the role
of sample size. Our results address the need of modern statistics to develop rigorously-justified
Laplace asymptotics that remain accurate in increasingly high-dimensional regimes. Indeed, high-
dimensional Laplace integrals are ubiquitous in statistics, arising as marginal likelihoods, model ev-
idences, or other posterior expectations. Complicating matters is the fact that these integrals must
often be evaluated repeatedly for different parameter choices, and then optimized over. Laplace
approximations such as the Bayesian Information Criterion are popular and extremely convenient
because they produce explicit approximations rather than black-box numerical estimates. Yet until
now, theoretical backing for these approximations was limited to the d2/λ →0 regime. Our results
push these guarantees into substantially higher-dimensional regimes, arbitrarily close to d/λ →0.
See Section 1.2 for more details and references.
Besides physics and statistics, high-dimensional Laplace-type integrals arise throughout sci-
ence and engineering, including in molecular simulation and theoretical chemistry (e.g., partition
functions and free-energy calculations) [42, 25], Bayesian inverse problems [13], and others.
Finally, our result may also be of interest in the theory of cumulants, since it solves the problem
of bounding the remainder in cumulant expansions. Obtaining a remainder bound using cumulant

theory (and the closely related theory of Gaussian chaos [15, 31, 30]) directly is deeply nontrivial.
We have avoided this problem by finding an alternative route. See Section 1.3 for more details.

precisely the loop corrections to mean field: Wick expansion of the Gaussian moments [16, Sections

Physics literature makes extensive use of truncations of (1.8) (or equivalent loop expansions).
However, derivations are usually done without rigorous error control [16, Sections 2.1 and 2.2] and
[6, Sections 1.1–1.3]. For finite d, the error control follows from classical asymptotic analysis [4, 44].
For growing d, such expansions have never been justified except for some specific cases.
In summary, mean field approximation and loop/cumulant expansions are central computational
tools in statistical physics, but rigorous remainder estimates (especially in large systems with many
degrees of freedom, d ≫1) are often absent from the standard presentations.
Our results provide a rigorous version of the loop-correction program for (1.4) in a joint limit
where both the dimension d (number of effective degrees of freedom retained in the reduced descrip-
tion) and the Laplace prefactor λ grow. Concretely, under mild confining assumptions ensuring
integrability and assuming a unique nondegenerate minimum x⋆together with bounded operator
norms of derivatives near x⋆, we obtain for every integer L ≥1 an expansion of log Zd(λ) through

can be classified as the latter task). These tasks become particularly demanding in the growing-
d regimes that now arise routinely in applications. For example, [14] tackles the above Laplace
optimization problem in the context of deep learning, where the integration is over the space of
hundreds of parameters of a neural network.
In this setting of optimizing an objective given by a high-dimensional integral, Laplace approxi-
mations are especially attractive. Indeed, they yield an explicit analytic surrogate for the objective
as a function of the tuning parameter, rather than the black-box numerical access provided by
methods such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo [33]. However, this convenience is only meaningful
when the approximation error is controlled in a dimension-dependent way. In fact, concerns about
the reliability of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) in high dimensions have been noted ex-
plicitly in the literature. The BIC is a further approximation on top of the Laplace approximation
used to simplify the optimization objective. In [10, p. 377], the authors write “for models with a
large number of predictors [large d] it is no longer clear that some version of the BIC, or perhaps
rather a Laplace approximation, accurately approximates a marginal likelihood”.
While recent work has begun to provide rigorous guarantees for high-dimensional Laplace ap-
proximations [37, 3, 39, 22], these typically require more restrictive scalings (at best, that d2/λ ≪1).
Our results push these guarantees essentially up to the concentration boundary, providing explicit
“error bars” for Laplace-based surrogates deep into the modern high-dimensional regime.

1.3
Connection to cumulant theory As stated above, the coefficients bk in (1.2) can be derived from formal cumulant expansions.
Although computing the coefficients using cumulants is easy, bounding the remainder in the ex-
pansion of log I(λ) using cumulant theory seems deeply nontrivial. Indeed, cumulants have been
thoroughly studied in the statistics, physics and combinatorics literature, yet rigorous remainder
bounds on cumulant expansions in the growing d regime are virtually non-existent.
This sug-
gests that bounding the remainder through the lens of cumulants may be intractable. In contrast,
our change-of-variables approach is not only tractable, but also avoids any heavy machinery, as
mentioned above.
Our remainder bound makes rigorous the well-known observation that, based on the terms,
expanding the cumulant generating function (cgf) is advantageous to expanding the moment gen-
erating function (mgf). Specifically, it is well-known that each cumulant is given by a sum of a
fewer number of summands than the corresponding moment. For example at the end of Section
3.10.1 of [26], McCullagh writes that the formula for cumulants is a sum only over “connected
pairs” of bi-partitions, as opposed to the formula for moments. In [16, Chapter 5.3], the author
writes, “When calculating cumulants, only fully connected diagrams (without disjoint pieces) need
to be included. This is a tremendous simplification.”
The simplification due to this summing over fewer terms in fact leads to a substantial improve-
ment. By analyzing the cumulants and moments directly in our setting, it is possible to show that
the moments that contribute to the kth coefficient in the I(λ) expansion are generically of order
O(d2k), while the cumulants that contribute to the kth coefficient in the log I(λ) expansion are
generically of order O(dk+1). Of course, this analysis of the terms does not rigorously prove any-
thing about the remainders. However, it naturally leads to the hypothesis that the remainders of

the two expansions behave like the first terms dropped from the truncation, i.e. that the Lth order
remainders scale as O(d2L/λL) and O(dL+1/λL), respectively. This is precisely what the previous
work [22] and the present paper rigorously prove.
Thus, as mentioned above, expanding the logarithm of I(λ) is what allows us to relax the d2 ≪λ
requirement in [22]. Given our result (1.2), it is very easy to see why d2 ≪λ is necessary for an
additive expansion of I(λ) to any order. Indeed, we have shown in (1.2) that, to leading order,
I(λ) = exp(O(d2/λ)). Thus if we Taylor expand the exponential to obtain an additive expansion
of I(λ), powers of d2/λ are present throughout. There is no finite number of terms we can subtract
from I(λ) to remove the dependence on d2/λ. However, we can remove the dependence on d2/λ
simply by dividing I(λ) by the single term exp(b1(f, g)/λ). The multiplicative remainder is then
of order exp(O(d3/λ2)).
In Section 8, we illustrate the power of expanding log I(λ) in two concrete examples: a quar-
tic perturbation of a Gaussian exponent, and a logistic-regression-type likelihood motivated by
statistics.

1.4
Related work There is a related body of work on Laplace-type integrals arising in statistics (e.g. posterior nor-
malizing constants and marginals) and statistical physics (partition functions) in the “proportional
asymptotics” regime.
Here, d/λ converges to a constant.
The regime lies beyond the concen-
tration threshold, so these integrals can only formally be considered to be Laplace-type. Due to
the lack of concentration, entirely different techniques are required to study such asymptotics.
See [24, 35, 7, 27, 28], as well as the referenced cited therein.

Organization The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the notation and conventions used through-
out. In Section 3, we describe the problem setting and state the main result, and Section 4 outlines
the proof strategy. Section 5 derives the expansion of the main contribution to the integral coming
from a neighborhood of zero, while Section 6 computes the expansion coefficients in terms of cu-
mulants. The tail contribution is estimated in Section 7. Section 8 compares our results with the
expansion of I(λ) in [22] and presents two applications of our expansion. Various technical results
are collected in the appendices.

4.1
Key steps Here, we outline the main ideas of our approach. Without loss of generality, we suppose x⋆= 0
is the global minimizer of f, with ∇2f(0) = Id, so that f(x) = ∥x∥2/2 + o(∥x∥2) near zero. For
simplicity, we only describe the proof in the case g ≡1. Incorporating a non-constant g presents
no real challenges. On the surface, our proof begins similarly to classical fixed-d proofs of the
expansion based on the Morse Lemma [44]. This lemma states that there is a change of variables
x = x(t) making the exponent f(x(t)) an exact quadratic. This approach, in its pure form, seems
to be intractable because the coordinate transformation from the Morse Lemma is difficult to work
with.

Instead, we use a variation of the approach. Fix any L ≥1.
Step 1: initial change of variables. We begin by constructing an explicit local polynomial
change of variables X(t), of the form X(t) = t + O(∥t∥2), t →0, which makes the exponent
“more quadratic” but not exactly quadratic. Specifically, it eliminates the third through (2L+1)st
order terms in the Taylor expansion of f around the minimizer, so that −λf(X(t)) = −λ ∥t∥2 /2 +
O(λ ∥t∥2L+2). But O(λ ∥t∥2L+2) is of order O(dL+1/λL) (and therefore negligible) in the region
∥t∥≲

The price we pay for this nice change of variables is the appearance of the Jacobian det(X′(t))
of the coordinate change, which we bring into the exponent. Thus the new exponential function is,
upon throwing out the negligible ∥t∥2L+2 term, given by exp(−λ∥t∥2/2+log det(X′(t))). Crucially,

In this section, we first compare our result to that of [22], and then apply our theory to a few
examples. Throughout the section, we assume d ≥log λ to simplify formulas.

8.1
Comparison to [22] In the below informal discussion of our results, we neglect any dependence on r0, κ. Since also
d ≥log λ, a constant R can be used to satisfy (3.8). Theorem 3.2 then shows that I(λ) =
 λ 2π

provided max1≤k≤2L ∥∇k(log g)(x)∥op < cg and max3≤k≤2L+2 ∥∇kf(x)∥op < cf, uniformly over x
in a small neighborhood of 0. As discussed in the introduction, in [22] it is shown that I(λ) =
 λ 2π

provided ∥∇kg(x)∥op ≲d⌈k/2⌉, k = 1, . . . , 2L and ∥∇kf(x)∥op ≲d⌈k/2⌉−2, k = 3, . . . , 2L + 2,
uniformly over x in a small neighborhood of 0. The suppressed constants in the big-O and in the
bound on |ak| in (8.2) depend on the suppressed constants in the derivative bounds.
Both results also require a few other minor assumptions, but we have highlighted the most
important ones for this discussion. We make a few comments on the difference between the two
works.

1. For (8.1), we have assumed the derivative operator norms are bounded independently of d.
This was not required in [22]. However, if the assumption does hold, then our result is strictly
stronger than that of [22]. Specifically, suppose the operator norms are bounded and d2 ≪λ.
Then (8.2) can be derived from (8.1).

2. As can be seen from [22], d2 ≪λ is necessary for the expansion of I(λ) even when the
derivative operator norms are bounded. Indeed, consider the example in Section 8.2, also
studied in Example 2.19 in [22].
We see that the derivative operator norms are indeed
bounded, yet [22] proves that the expansion of I(λ) is valid only if d2 ≪λ. This shows that
our improved dimension dependence cannot simply be attributed to the stricter requirement
we have imposed on the operator norms. Rather, it is due to the intrinsic difference between
expanding I(λ) and expanding log I(λ), as described in the introduction.

3. In the expansion of I(λ) in [22], the derivative operator norms beyond the fourth order were
allowed to grow with d due to slack in the bound. A similar phenomenon may hold for the
expansion of log I(λ). Namely, there may be some slack which would permit derivative norm
growth with d. We leave this investigation to future work.
